title: VAT Law and Compliance in Bangladesh – Complete Guide 2026
metaTitle: VAT Law & Compliance Bangladesh 2026 | Advocate Md. Shah Alam
metaDescription: Complete guide to VAT law in Bangladesh — VAT Act 2012, registration, rates, returns, penalties, and appeals. Expert legal advice from Dhaka.
heroIntro: Value Added Tax (VAT) is the single largest source of government revenue in Bangladesh, yet many businesses remain non-compliant due to complex registration and filing requirements. Whether you are a new entrepreneur or an established company, understanding the VAT Act 2012 and its regulations is essential to avoiding heavy penalties and legal action. This guide provides a thorough breakdown of VAT law, compliance obligations, and what to do when the National Board of Revenue (NBR) challenges your filings.
toc: Overview of the VAT Act 2012 in Bangladesh

sections.h2: Overview of the VAT Act 2012 in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Bangladesh's primary VAT legislation is the Value Added Tax and Supplementary Duty Act, 2012 (commonly called the VAT Act 2012), which came into full force on 1 July 2019 after several years of phased implementation. It replaced the older VAT Act 1991 and brought sweeping changes to the tax structure, compliance mechanism, and administrative framework of indirect taxation in the country. The Act is administered by the National Board of Revenue (NBR) under the Ministry of Finance.

The core philosophy of the VAT Act 2012 is a multi-stage, invoice-based VAT system where tax is collected at each stage of the supply chain — from manufacturing to wholesale to retail — with businesses entitled to offset the VAT they paid on inputs (input tax credit) against the VAT they collect on outputs. This prevents the cascading tax effect that plagued the earlier system.

In addition to VAT, the Act also governs Supplementary Duty (SD), which applies to luxury goods, tobacco, and certain services. Together, VAT and SD form the backbone of Bangladesh's indirect tax regime. Key subordinate legislation includes the VAT and SD Rules 2016 and various NBR general orders (SROs) that modify rates and exemptions periodically.

For businesses operating in Bangladesh, compliance with the VAT Act 2012 is not optional. NBR conducts regular audits, and failure to maintain proper VAT accounts, issue VAT invoices, or file timely returns can result in substantial fines, interest charges, and even criminal prosecution. Consulting a qualified company and corporate lawyer in Dhaka is strongly advised before commencing any business activity that may attract VAT liability.

sections.h2: VAT Registration: Who Must Register and How
sections.content:
Under the VAT Act 2012, any person engaged in an economic activity whose annual turnover exceeds the prescribed threshold must obtain VAT registration from the NBR. As of the current SRO provisions:

  • Mandatory VAT registration applies to businesses with annual turnover above BDT 3 crore (30 lakh) — these entities must charge VAT at the standard rate.
  • Businesses with turnover between BDT 50 lakh and BDT 3 crore fall under the Turnover Tax (TT) regime, paying a flat 4% turnover tax instead of full VAT.
  • Businesses below BDT 50 lakh are generally exempt, though voluntary registration is permitted.

The registration process is conducted through the NBR's online VAT registration portal (mushak.gov.bd). Applicants must submit:

  1. Application through BIN (Business Identification Number) system
  2. Trade license from the relevant city corporation or municipality
  3. TIN (Tax Identification Number) certificate
  4. National ID card of the proprietor/directors
  5. Bank account details
  6. Premises ownership or rental documents
  7. Description of goods/services supplied

Upon successful verification, the NBR issues a Business Identification Number (BIN), which is the VAT registration certificate. The BIN must be displayed at the business premises and quoted on all VAT invoices. Certain sectors — such as importers, manufacturers of excisable goods, and service providers in specified categories — must register regardless of turnover threshold.

Failure to register when required is a serious offence under Section 85 of the Act and can attract penalties equal to the VAT evaded plus additional fines. If you are unsure whether your business is required to register, consult an experienced tax lawyer in Bangladesh for a comprehensive assessment of your obligations.

Bangladesh applies VAT at multiple rates depending on the nature of the goods or services. The primary rates under the VAT Act 2012 are as follows:

  • Standard Rate: 15% — Applies to most taxable goods and services not specifically listed in the schedules.
  • Reduced Rates: Certain goods and services enjoy reduced VAT rates through scheduled exceptions — e.g., 5%, 7.5%, or 10% — which are updated annually through Finance Acts or SROs.
  • Zero Rate (0%): Exports of goods and certain specified services are zero-rated, meaning the business charges no VAT but can still claim input tax credits on purchases used to produce those exports.
  • Exempt Supplies: Listed in the First Schedule of the Act — these include essential food items, agricultural inputs, health services, educational services, and more. Suppliers of exempt goods cannot charge VAT but also cannot claim input tax credit.

Supplementary Duty (SD) is levied in addition to VAT on specified goods and services considered luxury or harmful — such as cigarettes, alcohol, certain vehicles, mobile phone services, and air-conditioned restaurants. SD rates range from 10% to 350% depending on the product category.

The Finance Act issued every year (typically in June, coinciding with the national budget) amends VAT rates and the schedules. Businesses must stay updated on these annual changes to ensure correct charging and claiming of VAT. Failure to apply the correct rate — whether charging too little or claiming unentitled credits — can trigger an NBR audit. A corporate lawyer familiar with tax law can help businesses conduct annual compliance reviews after each Finance Act is enacted.

sections.h2: Filing VAT Returns: Procedure and Deadlines
sections.content:
Registered VAT payers in Bangladesh are required to file monthly VAT returns using the prescribed Mushak-9.1 form (for standard VAT registrants) or Mushak-9.2 (for turnover tax payers). The return covers all transactions — both purchases and sales — during the calendar month.

Key Deadlines

The VAT return must be submitted to the relevant VAT Circle Commissionerate by the 15th day of the following month. For example, VAT return for January must be filed by 15 February. Payment of the net VAT liability must also be made by this deadline through treasury challans or electronic banking channels approved by NBR.

Documents and Records Required

  • All VAT invoices (Mushak-6.3) issued to customers during the month
  • Purchase invoices received from suppliers showing input VAT paid
  • Import entries (for importers) — Bill of Entry with VAT payment proof
  • Export documentation (for zero-rated supplies)
  • VAT account book (Mushak-13) maintained on a monthly basis

Filing Process

Returns are now filed electronically through the iBAS++ system or through the VAT online portal. Large taxpayers under the Large Taxpayers Unit (LTU) must file electronically without exception. Medium and small taxpayers may still file manually at the VAT Circle office in some jurisdictions, though NBR is progressively mandating online filing for all.

Late filing attracts automatic interest at 2% per month on unpaid VAT and a fixed penalty under Section 85. Businesses with complex supply chains or those dealing in both taxable and exempt supplies should prepare their returns carefully, as apportionment of input tax credit can be technically challenging. Seeking advice from a qualified tax lawyer in Dhaka before filing disputed or complex returns can prevent costly errors.

sections.h2: Input Tax Credit and Deduction Rules
sections.content:
Input Tax Credit (ITC) is the cornerstone of the VAT system — it prevents double taxation by allowing a registered business to offset the VAT it paid on its purchases (inputs) against the VAT it collects on its sales (outputs). Only the net difference is payable to the government. However, the rules governing ITC eligibility are strict and must be carefully observed.

Conditions for Claiming ITC

  • The business must be VAT registered with a valid BIN.
  • The purchase must be used in making taxable supplies — ITC cannot be claimed on purchases used for exempt supplies or personal consumption.
  • The supplier must have issued a proper VAT invoice (Mushak-6.3) containing their BIN, the VAT amount charged, description of goods/services, and the buyer's BIN.
  • The ITC must be claimed within 4 months of the relevant tax period (though this can be extended in certain circumstances).
  • The VAT paid must be verifiable through the NBR's online database if requested during audit.

Blocked ITC

Not all input VAT is creditable. The VAT Act 2012 and Rules specifically block ITC on certain categories of purchases including: entertainment expenses, motor vehicles used for non-business purposes, goods purchased for personal use of employees, and purchases from non-registered suppliers. Claiming ITC on blocked items is treated as tax evasion.

Mixed Supplies and Apportionment

Businesses making both taxable and exempt supplies must apportion their input tax on a fair and reasonable basis — typically based on the ratio of taxable turnover to total turnover. The methodology must be disclosed in the VAT return and agreed with the Commissionerate if the amounts are significant. Disputes over ITC apportionment are among the most common VAT litigation matters in Bangladesh, and having a competent corporate lawyer review your ITC position before audit can save substantial sums.

sections.h2: Penalties for VAT Non-Compliance in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The VAT Act 2012 contains a comprehensive penalty regime under Chapter Fourteen (Sections 85–97). Non-compliance can result in civil penalties, interest charges, and in serious cases, criminal prosecution. Below is a summary of the key penalties:

  • Failure to Register: Penalty up to the amount of VAT evaded during the unregistered period, plus interest at 2% per month.
  • Late Filing of Return: Fixed penalty of BDT 10,000 per return, plus 2% per month interest on unpaid VAT.
  • Failure to Issue VAT Invoice: Penalty of BDT 20,000 per instance or the amount of VAT involved, whichever is higher.
  • Incorrect Return: Where a return understates output VAT or overclaims ITC, the shortfall is recoverable with 2% per month interest plus a penalty of up to 50% of the understated amount.
  • Fraud and Tax Evasion: Under Section 92, wilful evasion of VAT is punishable by imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to 3 times the evaded amount.
  • Obstruction of VAT Officers: Refusing access to records or obstructing a VAT audit can result in immediate penalties and referral to law enforcement.

NBR also has powers to seize goods and premises, issue demand notices, and pursue recovery through certificate proceedings. In cases of large-scale fraud, NBR can file criminal complaints at the Customs, Excise, and VAT Commissionerate Courts.

If you have received an NBR demand notice, show-cause notice, or are under audit, it is critical to engage a tax lawyer in Bangladesh immediately. Responding incorrectly to an NBR notice can waive important legal rights and defences. Advocate Md. Shah Alam has experience representing clients before VAT authorities and appellate tribunals.

sections.h2: VAT Appeal and Dispute Resolution Process
sections.content:
When a business disputes an NBR assessment, demand, or penalty, the VAT Act 2012 provides a structured multi-tier appeal mechanism. Acting quickly is essential, as strict time limits apply at each level.

Step 1: Objection to the Commissionerate

The first step is filing a written objection (representation) to the Commissioner of VAT in the relevant Commissionerate within 90 days of the disputed assessment. A deposit of the undisputed portion of tax may be required. The Commissioner or an authorised officer will hear the case and issue a decision.

Step 2: Appeal to the Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal

If the business is dissatisfied with the Commissionerate decision, it can appeal to the Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal within 90 days of the order. The Tribunal, comprising senior judicial and technical members, hears evidence and legal arguments from both sides. Filing an appeal typically requires a pre-deposit of a percentage of the disputed amount (as specified by the court or as per NBR policy at the time of filing).

Step 3: Appeal to the High Court Division

Questions of law arising from Tribunal decisions can be appealed to the High Court Division of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh by way of a VAT Reference Case. This is strictly limited to legal questions — not fresh factual disputes. The High Court can affirm, modify, or reverse the Tribunal's order. In rare cases of urgency or fundamental rights violations, a Supreme Court lawyer may also file a writ petition challenging the legality of an assessment.

Alternative Resolution: Reconciliation and Settlement

NBR has introduced Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) for VAT disputes under Section 108A of the Act, allowing taxpayers to resolve disputes through a neutral facilitator without formal litigation. ADR is faster, confidential, and often results in mutually agreeable settlements. However, ADR is not available in cases involving fraud or criminal allegations.

Given the complexity of VAT law and the technical nature of tribunal proceedings, engaging an experienced tax lawyer in Dhaka from the outset of any dispute is strongly recommended to protect your legal and commercial interests.

faqs.question: What is the VAT rate in Bangladesh in 2026?
faqs.answer: The standard VAT rate in Bangladesh is 15% under the VAT Act 2012. Reduced rates of 5%, 7.5%, and 10% apply to specific goods and services. Exports are zero-rated, and certain essential goods and services are fully exempt. Supplementary Duty (SD) is levied on top of VAT for luxury and specified goods.

faqs.question: Who needs to register for VAT in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Any business with annual turnover exceeding BDT 3 crore must register for standard VAT. Businesses with turnover between BDT 50 lakh and BDT 3 crore pay Turnover Tax at 4%. Importers and manufacturers of excisable goods must register regardless of turnover. Registration is done online through the NBR portal and results in issuance of a BIN number.

faqs.question: What happens if I file my VAT return late in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Late filing of a VAT return attracts a fixed penalty of BDT 10,000 per return plus 2% per month interest on any unpaid VAT. Repeated non-filing can trigger an NBR audit, demand notice, and in serious cases, criminal proceedings for VAT evasion under Section 92 of the VAT Act 2012.

faqs.question: Can I appeal an NBR VAT demand notice?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can first file a representation (objection) to the Commissioner of VAT within 90 days. If unsuccessful, appeal to the Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal within another 90 days. Legal questions can be taken to the High Court Division by way of a VAT Reference. Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) is also available for eligible cases.

faqs.question: What is input tax credit and how does it work in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Input Tax Credit (ITC) allows VAT-registered businesses to deduct the VAT they paid on purchases (inputs) from the VAT they collect on sales (outputs). Only the net amount is paid to NBR. ITC is only claimable on purchases supported by valid VAT invoices (Mushak-6.3), used in taxable supplies, and claimed within 4 months of the relevant tax period. Purchases used for exempt supplies or personal use are blocked from ITC.

faqs.question: Is VAT applicable to online businesses and e-commerce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Online businesses and e-commerce platforms in Bangladesh are subject to the same VAT rules as physical businesses. If annual turnover exceeds the applicable threshold, VAT registration is mandatory. NBR has also issued specific guidance on VAT collection by digital platforms and non-resident digital service providers supplying to Bangladeshi consumers.
